walkthrough.title: Gather Supplies
walkthrough.text: Let's get Telestrations set up! First, place the word cards, the die, and the cleaning cloths in the center of the table where everyone can reach them. The group should agree on whether to use "This Side" or "That Side" of the word cards for this game. Both sides work the same way, but the words are different.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Sketch Books and Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one erasable sketch book, one dry-erase marker, and one word card. Write your name on the front cover of your sketch book. This is how everyone will know whose book it is when the books make their way around the table.

walkthrough.title: Roll for Your Secret Word
walkthrough.text: Everyone rolls the die at the same time. Match the number you rolled to the corresponding word on your card. Write that word secretly on the first page of your book. If the matching slot is blank, you get to make up any word you want. If it shows an underlined category, pick any word that fits that category.

walkthrough.title: Get Ready to Draw
walkthrough.text: Now flip to the first Sketch It page. In a four-player or six-player game, you will draw your own secret word first. In a five-player game, pass your book to the left immediately before drawing, so the person to your left draws your word instead. This ensures the round ends on a guess when books return to their owners. Everyone ready? Let's play!

summary.title: Gather Supplies
bullets: Place word cards, die, and cloths in center
Group agrees: "This Side" or "That Side" for word cards

summary.title: Hand Out Sketch Books and Markers
bullets: Each player: 1 sketch book + 1 marker + 1 word card
Write your name on the front of your sketch book

summary.title: Roll for Your Secret Word
bullets: Roll die → match number to word on your card
Write word secretly on first page
Blank slot = make up any word; underlined = pick from that category

summary.title: Get Ready to Draw
bullets: 4 or 6 players: draw your own word first
5 players: pass book left before first drawing
Flip to first SKETCH IT page and begin

walkthrough.title: The Telephone Chain
walkthrough.text: Telestrations works like a game of telephone, but with drawings and guesses. Each book follows a repeating cycle: someone writes a word, then the next person draws it, then the next person guesses what the drawing is, then the next person draws that guess, and so on. The fun comes from watching how the original word transforms as it passes through each player's interpretations.

walkthrough.title: Sketch It Pages
walkthrough.text: When you are on a Sketch It page, your job is to draw the current prompt. That prompt is either the original secret word or the previous player's guess. You have about one minute to sketch. Keep it simple and recognizable. There are two important restrictions: no letters and no numbers in your drawings. Stick to shapes, symbols, and pictures only.

walkthrough.title: Guess It Pages
walkthrough.text: When you are on a Guess It page, flip back to see the previous player's sketch, then write your best guess of what they drew. You must always write something, even if you have no idea. Silly guesses are fine and often make the reveal more entertaining. After writing your guess, flip forward to hide your answer.

walkthrough.title: Passing the Books
walkthrough.text: After each sketch or guess, flip the page forward to hide your work and pass the book to the player on your left. Everyone passes at the same time. This keeps the game synchronized and prevents anyone from peeking at pages they should not see. Keep the books moving at the same pace around the table.

walkthrough.title: The Big Reveal
walkthrough.text: When your own book comes back to you, it is time for the big reveal. Open your book from the beginning and flip through each page, sharing the chain with everyone. Watch how your original word evolved through sketches and guesses. This is the highlight of Telestrations. Go around the table so everyone gets to share their chain.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Options
walkthrough.text: You can use Friendly scoring or Competitive scoring. In Friendly mode, each player awards one point for their favorite sketch and one point for their favorite guess, plus you earn one point if the final guess in your book matches your original word. In Competitive mode, a guesser earns one point for matching the original word or the previous guess, and the sketcher earns one point if their drawing helped. Play three rounds total, and the player with the most points wins.

walkthrough.title: Game Length and Winning
walkthrough.text: Play exactly three rounds. After each round, erase your books with the cleaning cloths, draw new word cards, and start fresh. After all three rounds, add up everyone's points. The player with the most points wins. If there is a tie, the group decides the winner or shares the victory. Honestly, everyone wins in Telestrations because the laughs are the real reward.

summary.title: The Telephone Chain
bullets: Word → sketch → guess → sketch → guess → repeat
Books cycle through all players and return to owner
Humor comes from gradual information drift

bullets: Draw the current prompt (word or previous guess)
~1 minute per sketch, keep it simple
NO letters or numbers in sketches

Flip forward to hide your answer

bullets: Flip page → pass left → everyone passes simultaneously

summary.title: The Big Reveal

Go around so everyone reveals their chain

summary.title: Scoring Options
bullets: Friendly: 1 pt favorite sketch + 1 pt favorite guess + 1 pt self-match
Competitive: 1 pt correct guess + 1 pt helpful sketch + 1 pt self-match
Self-match = final guess matches your original word

summary.title: Game Length and Winning
bullets: Play exactly 3 rounds, erase books between rounds
Most points after 3 rounds wins
Ties: shared victory or group decides

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Table
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Telestrations! We will walk through one complete round with six players. Even if you have fewer players at your table, follow along to see how the game flows. By the end of this tutorial, you will know exactly how to play.

walkthrough.title: Everyone Gets a Word
walkthrough.text: Each player rolls the die, matches their number to a word on their card, and writes it secretly. Let's say Player one gets "pizza," Player two gets "sunflower," and the others get their own words. Remember, nobody shows their word to anyone else.

walkthrough.title: First Sketch
walkthrough.text: Flip to the first Sketch It page and draw your word. Player one draws a circle with triangles on top to represent pizza. Keep it big and simple. You have about one minute, so do not worry about making a masterpiece. When time is up, flip the page and pass your book to the left.

walkthrough.title: First Guess
walkthrough.text: Player two now has Player one's book. They flip back to see the sketch. It looks like a circle with triangles. Player two writes "pizza" on the Guess It page. Perfect match! But not every guess will be this clean. Flip forward and pass left again.

walkthrough.title: The Chain Continues
walkthrough.text: Now Player three has the book. They see the guess "pizza" and must draw it. Player three draws something that looks more like a pie than a pizza. Player four sees the sketch and writes "pie" instead of "pizza." And just like that, the telephone chain has drifted. This is where the magic happens.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Drawing Letters
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, Player five is struggling to draw "sunflower" and writes the letter S next to their flower sketch. Stop! No letters or numbers are allowed in sketches. This is the most common mistake new players make. Erase the letter and rely on your drawing alone. Arrows, circles, and stick figures are fine. Letters and numbers are not.

walkthrough.title: Recovery from the Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player five erases the S and instead draws a big sun above the flower to hint at "sunflower." That is much better. Use visual cues like context, size differences, and action lines instead of text. The restriction against letters is what makes the game fun and challenging.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Skipping a Page
walkthrough.text: Player three accidentally flips past the Guess It page and sketches on the next Sketch It page instead. Now there is a blank page in the chain, which breaks the flow. Let's catch that early. Always check whether the page says Sketch It or Guess It before you start. If you are supposed to guess, write words. If you are supposed to sketch, draw pictures.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Fix the Page Order
walkthrough.text: Player three erases their sketch from the wrong page, goes back to the correct Guess It page, and writes their guess there. No harm done as long as you catch it quickly. From now on, always check the page header before you begin.

walkthrough.title: The Books Come Home
walkthrough.text: After the books have traveled around the entire table, each book returns to its original owner. Do not peek yet! Wait until everyone has their book back, then take turns sharing your chains with the group.

walkthrough.title: The Hilarious Reveal
walkthrough.text: Player one opens their book. "Pizza" became "pie" which became "Pac-Man" which became "yellow circle eating dots." Everyone laughs. This is the best part of Telestrations. Go around the table so every player shares their chain. The weirder the drift, the better the story.

walkthrough.title: Score the Round
walkthrough.text: Let's use Friendly scoring for your first game. Each player gives one point for their favorite sketch in someone else's chain and one point for their favorite guess. If the final guess in your book matches your original word, give yourself one point too. Write down scores and get ready for round two.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have just played your first round of Telestrations! You have seen the sketch-guess-pass cycle, learned about common mistakes like drawing letters and skipping pages, and experienced the joy of the big reveal. Play two more rounds for a complete game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Draw Again
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your next round, here are three quick tips.

First, check out General Tips for advice on drawing clearly and guessing well.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to scoring and page types.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: Scripted tutorial with 6 example players
Follow along even with fewer players
Covers one complete round start to finish

walkthrough.title: Draw Big and Simple
walkthrough.text: The number one tip for Telestrations is to keep your drawings large and simple. Big shapes are easier to recognize than tiny detailed illustrations. If you are drawing a dog, a big oval body with four stick legs and a tail will communicate better than a realistic portrait. You only have about one minute, and the next person needs to identify your drawing at a glance.

walkthrough.title: Choose Concrete Words
walkthrough.text: When a blank or category slot lets you choose your own word, pick something concrete and easy to draw. Nouns like "tree," "car," or "hat" are much easier to sketch than abstract concepts like "freedom" or "confusion." The goal is to set up an entertaining chain, not to stump people into silence.

walkthrough.title: Guess the Obvious First
walkthrough.text: When you are guessing, start with the most obvious interpretation. If the drawing looks like a house, write "house" before trying to figure out if it might be a barn or a church. The simplest answer is usually what the sketcher intended. Overthinking leads to wild guesses that derail the chain early.

walkthrough.title: Always Write a Guess
walkthrough.text: Never leave a page blank, even if you have absolutely no idea what the drawing is. Write your best guess, even if it is silly or completely wrong. Blank pages break the chain and rob the group of the funniest moments. Some of the best Telestrations stories come from wildly wrong guesses that take the chain in unexpected directions.

walkthrough.title: Use Context Clues in Sketches
walkthrough.text: When your word is hard to draw, add context around it. Drawing "beach" is tough with just sand, but add waves, a sun, and an umbrella, and suddenly it clicks. Think about what scene your word belongs to and draw the whole scene rather than just the object. Arrows pointing to the key element can help too.

walkthrough.title: Keep the Pace Moving
walkthrough.text: Telestrations is a party game, and the energy dies when someone takes too long. Aim for about one minute per sketch. If you are not done, just pass what you have. An unfinished drawing still gives the next person something to work with. The most important thing is that everyone passes at the same time to keep the game synchronized and fun.

walkthrough.title: Embrace the Chaos
walkthrough.text: The best advice for Telestrations is to stop trying to win and start trying to laugh. The funniest moments happen when the chain goes completely off the rails. A perfect chain where every guess matches the original word is technically impressive but boring. The real joy is watching "flamingo" turn into "skinny pink alien" turn into "Barbie on stilts." Lean into the chaos and enjoy the ride.

bullets: Large shapes > tiny details for recognition
Stick figures and outlines work great
~1 minute per sketch — speed matters

bullets: For blank/category slots, pick easy-to-draw nouns
"Tree" or "car" > "freedom" or "confusion"
Goal: entertaining chain, not stumping people

bullets: Never leave a page blank — even silly guesses count
Blank pages break the chain and ruin the reveal

walkthrough.title: Strategic Sketching for Competitive Mode
walkthrough.text: In Competitive scoring, you earn a point when your sketch helps the next person guess correctly. This changes how you draw. Focus on clarity over creativity. Use universally recognized symbols. A stick figure with a cape means superhero to almost everyone. A circle with lines radiating out means sun. The more conventional your symbols, the more likely you earn that sketcher point.

walkthrough.title: Guessing for Points
walkthrough.text: In Competitive mode, you also earn a point when your guess matches either the original word or the previous guess. This means you should consider both possibilities. If the sketch could be "sun" or "star," think about what word was likely written before it. If the chain has been drifting, matching the immediate previous guess is often easier and still earns you the point.

walkthrough.title: The Self-Match Strategy
walkthrough.text: Getting the self-match point means the final guess in your book matches your original word. You can influence this by choosing words that are easy to draw clearly and that translate well through multiple rounds of telephone. Common, concrete nouns with distinctive silhouettes survive the chain better than abstract or ambiguous words. Think "guitar" rather than "music."

walkthrough.title: Reading the Room
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to who is sitting to your left. That person will be the first to interpret your sketch. If they are a literal thinker, draw literally. If they tend to overthink, keep your drawings extra simple to avoid misinterpretation. Adapting your drawing style to your audience is a subtle but effective edge in Competitive mode.

walkthrough.title: Variant: Switch Seats Between Rounds
walkthrough.text: Try switching seats between rounds so that different people interpret your drawings each time. This variant keeps the game fresh because the same drawing will be interpreted differently by different people. It also prevents two players who think alike from creating an unbreakable chain that scores easy self-match points every round.

walkthrough.title: Variant: Non-Dominant Hand
walkthrough.text: For an extra challenge, try sketching with your non-dominant hand. This levels the playing field between artistic and non-artistic players because everyone's drawings become equally wobbly. It also makes the reveals even funnier. This variant works best when the whole group agrees to do it for at least one round.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing the Fun Factor
walkthrough.text: Advanced Telestrations play is really about maximizing laughs. Create custom word cards with inside jokes, movie quotes, or themed categories for your group. Try reverse-passing — passing right instead of left — to shake up familiar patterns. The best groups treat Telestrations as a storytelling experience rather than a competition. The chains that produce the biggest laughs are the ones everyone remembers.

summary.title: Strategic Sketching for Competitive Mode
bullets: Clarity > creativity for earning sketcher points
Use universally recognized symbols
Stick figure + cape = superhero (everyone gets it)

summary.title: Guessing for Points
bullets: Match original word OR previous guess for 1 pt
Consider what the previous guess likely was
Matching immediate previous guess is often easier

summary.title: The Self-Match Strategy
bullets: Choose words with distinctive, easy-to-draw silhouettes
Concrete nouns survive the chain better than abstracts
"Guitar" survives; "music" drifts

summary.title: Reading the Room
bullets: Adapt drawing style to who sits to your left
Literal thinkers → draw literally
Overthinkers → keep drawings extra simple

summary.title: Variant: Switch Seats Between Rounds

entries.definition: A page in the sketch book designated for drawing. When you land on this page, draw the current prompt without using any letters or numbers.
A page in the sketch book designated for writing. When you land on this page, flip back to view the previous sketch and write your best guess of what it depicts.
A double-sided card with numbered word slots. Roll the die to determine which word you use. Cards have a 'This Side' and 'That Side' with different words.
An erasable booklet with alternating Sketch It and Guess It pages. Write your name on the front. Erase with the cleaning cloth between rounds.
The marker used for both drawing and writing in the sketch books. Works on the special pages and erases with the included cleaning cloths.

A casual scoring method. Each player awards one point for their favorite sketch and one point for their favorite guess in someone else's chain. You also earn one point if the final guess in your own book matches your original secret word.

A point-driven scoring method. A guesser earns one point if their guess matches the original word or the previous guess. A sketcher earns one point if their drawing helped the next guesser match. Self-match points also apply.
Earn one point if the very last guess written in your own book matches the original secret word you wrote on the first page. Available in both Friendly and Competitive scoring.
The word each player writes on the first page of their sketch book at the start of a round. Determined by rolling the die and matching the result to the word card.
The sequence of alternating sketches and guesses that travels through all players. The chain starts with a secret word and ends when the book returns to its original owner.
After each sketch or guess, flip the page forward and pass the book to the player on your left. All players pass simultaneously to keep the game synchronized.
The moment at the end of a round when each player flips through their returned book and shares the full chain with the group. This is the highlight of every round.
Letters and numbers are never allowed in sketches. Use only drawings, shapes, symbols, and visual cues. This core restriction is what makes the game challenging and fun.
If the die result matches a blank slot on your word card, you may invent any word of your choice as your secret word for that round.
If the die result matches an underlined category on your word card, choose any word that fits within that category as your secret word.
